summary: A biographical finding aid from the University of Chicago Library detailing John D. Rockefeller's role as a founding donor. It outlines his early business career, the formation of Standard Oil, and his transition into institutionalized philanthropy, highlighting his insistence on financial self-sustainability and thrift in his charitable giving.

principles.statement: Philanthropic endeavors must be financially self-sustaining.
principles.rationale: He believed institutions should not overreach their financial means, going so far as to halt donations to the University of Chicago until it cleared its deficit.

principles.statement: Giving is a heavy burden of decision making.
principles.rationale: He felt overwhelmed by the sheer volume of personal pleas for assistance, which eventually drove him to establish formal foundations to manage distribution decisions.

principles.statement: Avoid wasteful giving and ensure every penny is used to its greatest extent.
principles.rationale: He applied his shrewd, conservative business judgments and values of thrift to his philanthropic efforts.

heuristics: Never exhibit ostentatious behavior in style or manner.

anti_patterns.description: Operating a charitable institution at a deficit.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It demonstrates a lack of financial self-sustainability and an overreaching of financial means, which prompted Rockefeller to temporarily cut off funding to the University of Chicago.
